speaker: interviewer
text: --your time in Palestine and the War of Liberation Independence.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- at the time, it was already Israel. The War of Independence was over. And the UN has granted Palestine to be Israel and have their own-- own state called Israel. I-- I did go to work as a-- as a-- in the Air Force.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I was flying, I was working in-- in a-- in a-- in a machine shop, again, as a mechanic or as a machinist making [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what was it? It was, know, paint for planes. Those days, there was no jets yet. It was by planes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was in [INAUDIBLE]. And the headquarters also in [INAUDIBLE]. This was a small little villa taken away from-- I think, the Arabs used to live there. But in 1950 in 1950 or '51, I decided to go back to Germany to take care of my father's-- family's belongings and property in Merl, where I was born. That's what I did. And I ended up first in Cologne where, from my stepmother, a sister lived who was in Riga during the war, who was always a sickly person. And I-- I just couldn't understand that she was the [LAUGHS] one who came out of all that, where everybody else was healthy who did not.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it's kind of a-- a-- you know, sarcastic that things like this happened. I mean, she passed away now. You know, she's not alive anymore. But in Cologne, I met my wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: She came, Sonja, from the East Zone at that time in-- by Berlin, not far from Berlin. She ran away from there, because she couldn't stand the Communists. She wanted to be a-- a Zionist, you know, working, going to back to Israel. And there was no other way there. The-- the Communists wouldn't allow anything like that. For them, was to be a Communist, you do what we tell you and-- and nothing else. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you met Sonja, your future wife, in Cologne?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In Cologne. She ran away. She-- she ran away and ended up in Cologne. And she is a-- was a-- how they call-- a hairdresser. And I worked down below in the machine shop as a machinist. And she walked by. And I looked at her. I guess it was love at first sight. [LAUGHS] So I-- I went to the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the-- the head of the school, and whatever his name was. I forgot his name. [? Baer, ?] I think it could have been. And I went up there. I had some kind of an excuse I-- I took. And here she was sitting. And-- and they gave her something to eat. And we looked at each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: And right away on the first day, I told her-- well, I'm Sonja. I'm Alfred, I'm Fred. Can we have a date? All right. Fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she-- she made money. But I didn't. Because she worked-- she didn't live-- she lived with a-- an [NON-ENGLISH] they called it. You know, there's another person in the same apartment with a-- a non-Jewish woman.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she worked as a beautician. And she made money. And when I came from Israel, the food over there wasn't so plentiful. And every-- every time we stopped at a-- like another hamburger stand or a bratwurst stand-- do you know what bratwurst is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those-- those little [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] wieners, but, you know, warmed up right on the outside. And-- and she-- you know, she bought it all for me. And, finally, we got married. And-- and--

speaker: interviewer
text: After how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we-- after long-- we knew each other not even a half a year, we got married. OK. So then we contacted the people, friends from my father. And their name is [? Hauff, ?] [? Ida ?] and [? Julius ?] [? Hauff, ?] who were dear friends. I wrote to them. And they said right away, yes. We'll take you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did--

speaker: interviewee
text: Or, tell us we will be in Cologne on this and this day. Then you can tell us. We come there. We're going to come by boat. We have a car.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, what kind of a car? An Oldsmobile. An Oldsmobile? I didn't know what an Oldsmobile was. So we met in front of the Cathedral. And here they came. And they took us out for dinner. And after they left, right away I wrote them, yes, Julius. I would like-- we would like to come. So I said, right away, yes. Come to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Come.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they lived in Erie, Pennsylvania?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And they're sent us the papers. They had connections in-- in the government in Erie, the state government. And it didn't took long. We had our certificate. And we went. We went from Cologne drove down to-- to Amsterdam, shipped in. And we ended up in Hoboken, New Jersey.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- wait a minute. We came here 1954.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember what-- what day that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: In was in April the 5, I think, 1954.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have children at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about your children.

speaker: interviewee
text: She's sitting right there, Rita. We came with her. And she was two years old that time. She was a beautiful baby. Everybody stopped.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah. What? Where is she? She ain't listening. What a nice, what a beautiful baby. And she had-- what she had on, you know, little ears, like looks like-- a-- no. I don't know what they call that, Easter bunny or something. And we stood there in-- in New York. My cousin, who I was-- was also in the-- in the Navy, we met him there, too. And he took us around. We went to Radio Music Hall. And the-- the-- what is the tower? What is it called? The Empire State Building.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Empire State Building.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you think about finally being in America after all you had been through?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I felt good. I did feel really good. I said, that's the new world. That's a new beginning. And we finally got to-- to Erie by train. And they picked us up. And first, we lived in a small little apartment. And there was-- we lived in the front. In the back, there was another couple, older couple, Jewish people, too. They had television. We didn't. Every time they called us, come on, come on. It's television on. We have to watch this. And [? Ida ?] [? Hauff, ?] they had many supermarkets. And they-- every time they came and they opened up the refrigerator, and said, hey, there's nothing in it. Come on. Let's go. We need something. You are not eating. And they brought everything. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have another child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the other child is Eddie. Well, we didn't have him till 1957.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you living when you had him?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were still living in Erie.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then where did you move to after Erie, Pennsylvania?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came here in this crazy town.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Los Angeles, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you've lived here ever since?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ever since. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience any anti-Semitism in this country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there are. They are. There is. There is. I even had a-- i had a little [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] thing going this morning in the store. Some guy, he's-- he's Latin. And he looked at the [NON-ENGLISH]. And there was-- there was something written about Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he-- he started talking that Israel, well, they're no good. They're-- you know, first I didn't know what he was talking about. And I got so upset. I-- I said no. What do you know about it? If it wouldn't have for Israel, you wouldn't have no place to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: If-- if-- if Israel would of been established during World War II, Hitler wouldn't have done what he did. Because he wouldn't be alive today. Oh, they would-- they would have killed him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it still happens today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it does.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how does it make you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: It does not make me feel very well. And I-- I try to fight it back. And sometimes I showed him the number. Ah, he said, that's the ghetto. I said, that's not ghetto. That's something else. Yeah, I know. I said, ghetto and this are two different things.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he wouldn't listen. And there was a black fellow who we know comes to the store every day. He said, he's a kid. He doesn't know. What does he know? And it's true, the Latins are-- and that-- OK. How does it feel to give your testimony today? By giving your testimony what do you hope to accomplish?

speaker: interviewee
text: I feel-- yeah, I hope that people will see what it really was like, what Hitler did, what the Germans did. Even they themselves, some of them might have not go along with it. Some of them didn't even know. But they were so involved that-- that that was taboo for them, what Adolf said that they had to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: And even today they always come, well, I just did, because Hitler told me so even it was wrong. Even, they knew inside that it-- that it was against normal-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] normal living. I mean, normal person, people will not-- will not accept anything like. Where-- where was liberty? There's no liberty and was no liberty in Germany. What do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was nothing but depressed, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think helped you to survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: What? What?

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think helped you to survive--

speaker: interviewee
text: Helped me survive?

speaker: interviewer
text: --everything that you went through?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that each-- each thing that happened to me, it-- it had a little twist to it. And I-- I-- I felt trying to-- to-- to-- to get away from it or to try to-- to make the best of it and-- and also look for another escape to get away to-- to-- even when it started with the [NON-ENGLISH] in-- in-- in-- in Auschwitz, when I came there, and even when it started in-- in Mauthausen, with this fellow who-- who wanted to so much to work in the crematorium. And I don't know, maybe it's a sixth sense I had not to do this or not to do that and be careful, to- to go step by step. And it's no, no. You know, it's-- it's not a real-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] real something really what-- what in-- in real medicine what you say you would take and to get away from all this. But, you know, everything had a good side and a bad side. If it this was stupid boots, if it was the pair of shoes I picked up in Mauthausen with the gold and with the diamonds in it and all that, that all helped to-- to give me confidence to-- to feel that there might be a way I'm going to live and get out of it. But during the time I was in, I-- I had a-- a false-- I-- I never thought I would ever make it out. I-- I-- my-- my thoughts were so that I had to have something to hold onto it. And I began to think that there is a second life after death. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I think different today. But there I thought about that. There is a second life after death. So you're going to be OK. You die. But you're going to go like in Heaven, somewhere to a better-- better place.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe your faith today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I think today you have to do your duties as long as you live and be alive. There-- once you die, there's nothing anymore. You try. And that's part of the Jewish faith, I think. There-- some rabbis may say, well, there's another world. But I think that today is the time to do your duty and do what you can as long as you're alive to help your children or help your fellow man somehow and maybe you yourself, too. But that's what is important, to-- to do everything now as long as you're alive. After that, there won't be nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. We'll introduce your family now. Mr. Wolf, will you introduce us to your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My daughter, Rita, Rita Wolf. Been together for quite some years. [LAUGHTER] We don't mention any years--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: On women, on girls.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a rule.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you can-- you can-- everybody can see that you look good. You--

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: --do good job. You work for Aetna. It's an insurance company. You also have a good voice and try in spare time maybe to do a little singing, nightclub singing. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, that you're-- now that you're here with your-- with your daughter, is there some message that you would like to-- to give us for future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it would be good for her, also, and the rest of generations to-- to listen to what we as Jews went through and that it-- that it is a warning for everyone in the world today that this could happen again and to be look out for people who not only are Jews, but other minorities trying to-- to put them down or-- or-- just look at them as [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not very full-- full-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] full people to-- to-- who are-- do just as good a job for the world and for themselves and for their children as if they are black, or they're brown, or white.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Rita, how-- how has it been for you to be a second generation survivor? What can you tell us about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Actually, that's another four or five tapes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was some-- there was difficult aspects to it. And there are some positive ones. The difficult ones are you grow up with not the experience directly, but how something so devastating has affected someone so close to you, someone who's so related-- that's that closely related to you and how they assimilate those experiences and how they also use-- how it affects their lives in the way they raise their children and the way they do their business every day. And-- and you see that the older you get, that some things that they do is a direct impact of what they went through. And that nobody had any control over it. And-- and sometimes, it-- it comes out in a good way. And sometimes it comes out in a bad way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there something you'd like to tell your dad now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I [POINTS] want to stop this. Can we stop?

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I can just ask you about your faith today. I asked your dad. And just about--

speaker: interviewee
text: About my faith?

speaker: interviewer
text: About your faith. I asked your dad how he-- what his faith is like now after everything he's been through. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I'm-- not-- I'm not that strong a believer in-- in organized religion.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [BACKGROUND NOISE] Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it? Jesus. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Wolf, who do we see in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm on that photo and also the rest of my class, school class. We were on an outing. And it probably was in 1935, '36. We were-- well, I was 11 years old. And we, you know, joking around. And especially there's one girl in a white dress standing up holding her hands in front of her, her name is Clementina. And-- and she is-- she's a good friend of mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where are you in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am sitting right below her and looking at everybody, smiling. I am on this picture standing up smiling. It's on the ice on the-- on the Moselle River. We are, you know, standing there, whatever. The girl up front is a-- a very close friend from school. And behind her sits her brother on the sled who passed away in the meantime. So it was about the same time in 1935 on a-- you know, it could be on a Sunday when everybody wasn't working and had time to-- to, you know, do a little outing. The fellow standing up is me, Manfred Wolf. The girl sitting on the sled, her name is Clementina Shait. But today her name is [? Berger. ?] She was married. And behind her is Edward, her brother who has passed away in the meantime. That's me, Manfred Wolf, with my wife, Sonja. Sonja Wolf [NON-ENGLISH] Berger. It was on our wedding day in 1953 about in-- oh, about in August 1953.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which city?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Cologne.

speaker: interviewer
text: Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Germany. Oh, sorry. This is a picture of myself, Manfred Wolf, my wife, Sonja, and my newborn daughter, Rita Wolf. And that was in a park in Cologne, Germany in 19-- 3-- 19-- 1953. This picture is myself, Manfred Wolf, with my wife Sonja Wolf. And the little daughter, Rita Wolf was about two, three years old in Erie, Pennsylvania--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the United--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the United States. OK. That's me, Manfred Wolf with my wife, Sonja Wolf, and the little daughter, Rita Wolf. The country was in the United States, 1954 in Erie, Pennsylvania.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is my wife, Sonja Wolf, and daughter, Rita Wolf, in 1958 on Lake Erie in the United States. This is my wife, Sonya Wolf, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Schwarzwald in Germany in 1957, '58. No?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cut. No, no. In the '70's, 1970's.

speaker: interviewee
text: '70. OK. This is myself, Manfred Wolf, my wife, Sonya Wolf, in the backyard barbecuing for the family. All that happened in Malibu about 1978.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the United States. In Malibu.

speaker: interviewer
text: In California?

speaker: interviewee
text: In California.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who do we see in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a friend of mine, a school colleague. Her name is Clementina Shait.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Born-- born [? Berger. ?] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was taken in Merl, Germany in 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who do we see on the right in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's my son, Edward Wolf. And on the left my granddaughter, Ariella Wolf. The picture was taken in San Diego outside the zoo or somewhere. And it was taken in 1998.

speaker: interviewee
text: No--

speaker: interviewer
text: In California in the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: In California in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Wolf, who do we see in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right is my grandchild, Zach. He's two years old. And on the left is Ariella. She's five years old. It's done in front of their home in Santa Monica, California.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year?

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is in Santa Monica, California in the United States. Mr. Wolf, who do we see in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right is my son, Edward Wolf. On the left is my daughter, Rita Wolf. It was done here in Santa Monica, 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Mr. Wolf, who do we see here on the left and right?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right is my daughter-in-law, Debbie, [? Debra ?] Wolf. And the left is my daughter, Rita Wolf.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this photo made?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was made here in Santa Monica, 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: In California?

speaker: interviewee
text: In California.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is this village?

speaker: interviewee
text: This village is called Merl on the Moselle in Germany. It's my home town. And the Moselle River running right in front of it. This picture was taken in 1994 in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your house in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: My house-- the picture is too small to get my home on there. If the picture would have been taken on a little further to the right, we would have seen my home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
